title: Simple cotton dishcloth
subtitle: Six rows of treble, fasten off
excerpt: The first crochet project most beginners finish. A square of treble crochet in DK cotton, six rows, an evening's work, washes the dishes from the next morning onward.
sourceNotes: Weldon's Practical Crochet (multiple late-Victorian volumes) — kitchen cloth patterns worked in mercerised cotton, the direct ancestor of the modern crochet dishcloth. Beeton's Book of Needlework (1870) — square-flat-fabric construction reference. Pauline Turner's modern method notes on DK cotton tension cross-referenced for the contemporary phrasing.

crochet.gaugeText: 18 trebles × 10 rows = 10 × 10 cm in DK-weight cotton on a 4 mm hook, unblocked. The dishcloth is a working textile and isn't blocked.
crochet.finishedSizeText: Approximately 22 × 22 cm — a generous palm-and-fingers wash cloth that fits the bottom of a kitchen sink.

recipeTools.notes: For sewing in the starting and finishing tails.
Useful for checking gauge before you commit to the full six rows.

glossaryTerms.definition: The starting chain of any flat crochet piece. The first row of trebles is worked into this chain; the chain doesn't appear in the finished piece, but its length determines the width of the cloth.

content.text: The cotton dishcloth is the first finished piece most beginners make. Six rows of treble, two evenings' work, and the cloth is in use by the next morning — washing the dishes, drying the worktop, lasting a year or two before retirement to the floor-cleaning drawer. The Victorian originals were worked in mercerised crochet cotton at much finer gauge; the modern version uses DK-weight kitchen cotton at a hook that makes for a single-evening project.

STITCH tutorial, this pattern is a graduation piece: the same treble worked back and forth across six rows. No magic ring (this is flat construction), no chart (the prose is shorter than a chart would be), no joining (it's one piece). Six rows, fasten off, sew in two ends, wash the dishes.

attrs.title: Materials
attrs.body: About 50 g of DK-weight cotton. Cotton holds up to repeated machine-washing in a way that wool doesn't — for a dishcloth it's the only reasonable choice. 4 mm crochet hook (UK 8, US G/6). Tapestry needle. Scissors. Total cost: well under £5 for a cloth that'll last a year. The dishcloth is the canonical "crochet pays for itself" project.

content.text: 18 trebles × 10 rows = 10 × 10 cm in DK-weight cotton on a 4 mm hook, unblocked. The dishcloth is a working textile and isn't blocked — gauge as you go. If your row of 18 measures more than 11 cm, switch to a 3.5 mm hook; if less than 9 cm, switch to a 4.5 mm hook. The finished cloth wants a moderately firm tension — too loose and the cloth feels droopy; too tight and the cotton doesn't absorb water.
treble (tr) — UK convention; the same stitch as US "double crochet".
slip stitch (sl st) — UK and US shared. Used only for finishing, not for working rows.
Worked flat, turning the work at the end of each row. The 3-chain turning chain counts as the first treble of the row. Numbers in brackets at the end of each row are the running stitch count.

is 42 chains: the 40 worked stitches the cloth wants plus 2 extra. (The 2 extra cover the 3-chain turning chain that counts as the first treble, minus one because the first worked treble lands in the fifth chain from the hook — the maths is consistent across every flat-treble pattern.)
Ch 3 (counts as the first tr — but the chain is already part of your 42-chain foundation). Work 1 tr into the 4th chain from the hook, and 1 tr into each of the remaining chains. (40 sts.)

Turn the work. Ch 3 (counts as the first tr). Skip the first stitch of the row below (the previous turning chain). Work 1 tr into each of the next 38 stitches. Work the last tr of the row into the top of the previous turning chain — this is the second canonical pattern beat to learn and the one most beginners forget. (40 sts.)
Repeat row 2 four more times. At the end of row 6, fasten off.

Fasten off, leaving a 15 cm tail. Sew the starting tail and the finishing tail into the wrong side with a tapestry needle — 3-4 stitches in one direction, then 2-3 stitches back the other way to lock the tail. Trim. The cloth doesn't need blocking; the trebles relax into shape after the first wash.

Machine-wash with the rest of the kitchen linen at 40°C — DK cotton stands up to hot water and detergent without losing shape. Tumble-dry low or line-dry. Replace the cloth when the trebles start to flatten and the fabric loses its sponginess — typically after twelve months of daily use.

attrs.heading: If your dishcloth doesn't come out square
attrs.intro: Three things that go wrong on a first dishcloth, and the fix for each.

items.symptom: The cloth gets narrower row by row
items.cause: You missed the top of the previous turning chain at the end of each row. Each row lost one stitch.
items.fix: On every row from row 2 onward, the last treble of the row is worked into the top of the previous turning chain — that chain counts as a stitch even though it doesn't look like one until you've worked into it. Count to 40 at the end of each row.

items.symptom: The cloth gets wider row by row
items.cause: You worked into both the top of the previous turning chain and the last treble — adding a stitch each row.
items.fix: Skip the first stitch of the row below (the previous turning chain) at the start of each row. The first worked treble of row 2 lands on the second stitch of row 1.

items.symptom: The cloth comes out floppy and absorbs poorly
items.cause: Tension too loose; or the cotton you bought is the very softest mercerised kind, which is gorgeous in the hand but doesn't hold water.
items.fix: Try one hook size down. If the cloth is still floppy, the cotton is too fine — kitchen-cotton labels often say "dishcloth weight" or "DK kitchen cotton"; the heavier kind is what you want for dish-washing duty.

content.text: A second dishcloth in a different colour — the canonical second project. A face flannel in the same pattern but worked in cotton-and-bamboo blend instead of pure cotton. Or the next step up the pattern ladder: the granny square, which uses the same treble in a corner-and-cluster construction instead of a flat row.
